claim: Superconducting: ~70% chance of reaching fault-tolerant, useful-scale QC (~10-15 yr). Most mature, most capital, fastest gates, error correction already improving with scale; multiple serious players de-risk it. Loose forecast, independent per-path odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if modular interconnect and logical scaling keep working; down if surface-code overhead proves intractable

claim: Trapped ion: ~60%. Highest demonstrated quality (accuracy, coherence, ~2:1 physical-to-logical ratio); open question is scaling qubit count, not quality. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a chip-based ion system scales past hundreds of qubits with fidelity intact

claim: Neutral atom: ~58%. Fastest recent progress, leads in error-corrected qubit count, cheap identical atoms, software-reconfigurable connectivity; held back mainly by relative youth. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if sustained large logical-qubit operation at higher fidelity is demonstrated

claim: Silicon spin: ~45%. Best manufacturing/scaling story (rides the semiconductor industry), just hit top-tier quality on an industrial line; but fewest qubits so far and no logical-qubit demo yet. High variance. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a first credible logical qubit is built on silicon spin

claim: Photonic: ~35%. Room-temperature operation and native networking are real advantages, but gated almost entirely by unsolved optical loss; many gates probabilistic, needing heavy redundancy. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if optical loss drops below the fault-tolerance threshold with a logical qubit demonstrated

claim: Topological: ~15%. A bet on an unproven physics claim; the existence of the qubit itself is disputed by much of the community; low expected value precisely because the existence question is open. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: independent reproduction of an accepted topological qubit; could collapse toward 0 or jump materially

claim: Annealing/D-Wave gate-model pivot: ~30% (aside, not ranked head-to-head). Present-day annealing optimization usefulness is a separate, already-banked win answering a different question. Loose forecast, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if the gate-model line delivers logical qubits on its roadmap

claim: Author thesis (tracked forecast): the long-term endpoint of quantum computing is optical/photonic rather than electronic. The author's conviction after years of research, which currently runs ahead of the demonstrated evidence (which ranks photonic lower today, ~35%); conviction and demonstration are different columns of the ledger.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: photonic optical loss below the fault-tolerance threshold with a working logical qubit; if it hits, the photonic probability (QCF5) revises up materially

claim: NV centers (diamond): ~12%. Physics is strong but the case for scalable general-purpose computing is weak — placing and wiring many NV qubits accurately in diamond is the unsolved problem, and the field has largely converged on NV as excellent for sensing, networking, and communication rather than a path to a general fault-tolerant computer. The ~12% measures the fault-tolerant, useful-scale COMPUTING question specifically; NV may succeed strongly in a different lane (sensing/networking) that this number does not measure. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a credible multi-qubit error-corrected NV processor architecture with a real scaling path appears (not another sensing/networking milestone)

claim: III-V quantum dots (optically-active, photon sources): ~10-12%. Physics is strong but as a route to a general fault-tolerant computer it is weak — dot-to-dot variability and the fact they function as a COMPONENT feeding photonic/network architectures rather than a self-contained processor. The ~10-12% measures the fault-tolerant, useful-scale COMPUTING question specifically; III-V dots may succeed strongly in a different lane (best-in-class on-demand single-photon sources; spin-photon interfaces for networking) that this number does not measure. Loose forecast, independent odds, +/-~15 pts, mid-2026.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: up if a credible scalable processor architecture built on III-V quantum dots appears (not merely an improved photon-source benchmark)

claim: Hefei anchors China's effort: the National Laboratory for Quantum Information Sciences (set in motion after Xi's 2016 USTC visit) and the 'Quantum Avenue' cluster on Yunfei Road (30+ companies). Verified construction figure ~CNY 7B (~$1B) Phase 1 per MERICS; 'world's largest facility' is a reported characterization.

Pan Jianwei / USTC milestones: Micius satellite first space-to-ground QKD + ~2,032 km Beijing-Shanghai QKD backbone; Jiuzhang photonic boson-sampling advantage (3.0 ~255 photons, 2023); Zuchongzhi superconducting processors (62 to ~176 qubits). Communication and sampling-advantage milestones, not fault-tolerant computing.

Private pullback, state effort still rising: Alibaba (Nov 2023) and Baidu (Jan 2024) closed their quantum labs and pivoted to AI, but the overall Chinese effort grew — state-aligned specialists (Origin Quantum, QuantumCTek) took over commercially, the 2026 15th Five-Year Plan names quantum an industrial priority, a national guidance fund earmarked ~CNY 121.8B, and Q1-2026 financing (~CNY 2.2B) nearly matched all of 2025.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (DCD/IEEE Spectrum/HPCwire/quantumzeitgeist/The Quantum Insider)

claim: The widely-cited ~$15.3B China national quantum investment figure is not verifiable: it traces to a single 2022 McKinsey 'announced government investment' estimate with thin-China-data caveats; same-lineage figures range $15B-$150B; an insider estimates real spend at perhaps a third.
verifier: open — signpost: locate/确认 an auditable primary source broken down by program

claim: The author's recollection of '~100+ institutions each meant to parallel or overtake MIT/Stanford' is unverified in that exact form; it appears to merge two separate documented policies (see CN7): Double First-Class (42 world-class / 140 institutions, world-leading by 2050) and the 12-university 'Schools of Future Technology' program explicitly framed to RIVAL (documented word, not 'surpass') MIT and Stanford. Stated at the strength sources support, no higher.
verifier: open — signpost: locate/确认 a single primary policy stating ~100 institutions intended to surpass MIT/Stanford; absent that, the accurate claim is the two documented programs (CN7)

claim: Author argument (the author's view, reframed): the question is not whether the West has already lost, but whether US/allied quantum investment and STEM-pipeline building are large enough and EARLY enough to keep pace with a still-accelerating two-decade Chinese program. A wake-up call, not an obituary; counter-evidence held in the same breath — the $15B is unverifiable, much of China's lead is in communication, and most assessments still put the US ahead in quantum computing.
verifier: open forecast — signpost: eased if the US/allies sustain a reauthorized fully-funded national quantum program and are first to fault-tolerant useful-scale QC; sharpened if China is first to fault-tolerant useful-scale QC or fields an operational unbreakable continental quantum network while Western funding stays stop-start

claim: Documented Chinese elite-STEM-university drive: the Double First-Class initiative (双一流, 2015; 2017 lists) named 42 'world-class' universities and 465 first-class disciplines across 140 institutions, aiming to lead the world's best by 2050; separately, the Ministry of Education named 12 universities to 'Schools of Future Technology' explicitly to rival MIT and Stanford in science and technology.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (Wikipedia/THE/University World News/SCMP)

claim: US response lag: the US National Quantum Initiative (2018) saw its core R&D authorization lapse on 30 Sep 2023, with reauthorization stalled in committee through 2024 and only re-advancing in 2026; a federal panel urged a 'Quantum First by 2030' goal (Nov 2025) and industry (Microsoft) warned the US risks falling behind and urged a larger STEM-talent pipeline.
verifier: citation_audit.md — reported, audit pending (FedScoop/The Quantum Insider/Congress.gov/CNBC)
